data.page_subtitle: Build Your Cyberdeck. Meet Your People.

children.text: cyberdeck.club is a community platform for cyberdeck builders — part wiki, part forum, part build showcase. Whether you're building your first portable computer or you've been at it for years, this is your home base.

A cyberdeck is a portable, often custom-built computer system designed for personal use. Think of it as a blend between vintage portable computing and modern maker culture. They're functional, they're expressive, and they're deeply personal projects that combine electronics, software, and design.

We're builders, hackers, makers, and enthusiasts who share a passion for portable computing and custom hardware. Our community includes beginners starting their first build with a Raspberry Pi and a dream, experienced builders refining their craft over years of iteration, electronics enthusiasts soldering and debugging, software developers writing custom OS configurations, and designer-makers creating beautiful enclosures.
What unites us is the desire to build computers that reflect who we are — not just what manufacturers think we should use.
Start with the Wiki: New to cyberdeck building? Our wiki has guides covering everything from component selection to soldering techniques.
Join the Forum: Have questions? Want to share your progress? The forum is where the conversation happens.
Share Your Build: Built something cool? The Builds section is for showcasing your work and inspiring others.
Attend Meetups: We hold regular virtual meetups and occasional in-person events.
cyberdeck.club is built with Astro and EmDash CMS. It's designed to be fast, accessible, and easy to contribute to. The source is open, and the community drives the content.

data.bio: DIY electronics enthusiast. Building cyberdecks since 2019. Lost one to a soldering accident, built three more.
Software developer by day, cyberdeck builder by night. Currently working on a mesh networking build.
If it can't be soldered, it's not worth building. 15+ years in electronics repair.
Repurposing old electronics into portable powerhouses. Pelican case enthusiast.

data.title: Getting Started with Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: Everything you need to know to start building your first cyberdeck. A beginner's comprehensive guide.
data.author: $ref:byline-neonpunk

children.text: Welcome to the cyberdeck.club wiki! This guide will walk you through everything you need to know to start building your first cyberdeck. No prior experience required.
What is a Cyberdeck?

A cyberdeck is a portable, often custom-built computer system designed for personal use. Think of it as a blend between a vintage portable computer and modern maker culture. They're functional, they're expressive, and they're deeply personal.
Essential Components
Before you start building, you'll need to understand the core components:
Compute Module: The heart of your system. Raspberry Pi, Orange Pi, or Intel NUC are popular choices.
Display: From small LCDs to e-ink panels, your display defines the character of your build.
Input: Mechanical keyboards are a staple, but trackballs and custom input devices are also common.
Power: Battery management is critical. LiPo and 18650 cells are the most common choices.
Budget Guide
You can build a functional cyberdeck for as little as $75-150, or spend $500+ for a premium build. Start simple, iterate later.

data.title: Your First Cyberdeck: A Step-by-Step Build
data.excerpt: Walk through building a beginner-friendly cyberdeck using a Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W and a compact LCD.
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: This guide assumes you have basic tools: a soldering iron, wire cutters, and some patience. Let's build your first cyberdeck together.
Bill of Materials
Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W - $25, 3.5" LCD (SPI) - $20, Power bank 5000mAh - $15, Mechanical keyboard switches x20 - $10, 3D printed case (or project box) - $15, Wires, buttons, and odds and ends - $10
Assembly
Start by testing all components individually before assembly. Connect the LCD to the Pi's SPI pins, verify it works, then proceed to the keyboard matrix.

data.title: Pelican Case Cyberdecks: A Popular Enclosure Choice
data.excerpt: Why Pelican cases make excellent cyberdeck enclosures and how to mod them for your build.
data.author: $ref:byline-cabinetx

children.text: Pelican cases are the gold standard for portable protection. They're waterproof, crush-proof, and come in sizes perfect for cyberdeck builds.
Recommended Models
Pelican 1060: Micro builds, Pi Zero sized. Pelican 1080: Small builds, Pi 4 footprint. Pelican 1490: Full keyboard + display builds.
Essential Mods
You'll need to cut holes for your display, keyboard, and any ports. A rotary tool with a carbide cutter makes quick work of the hard plastic.

data.title: Raspberry Pi Compute Platforms Compared
data.excerpt: A detailed comparison of Raspberry Pi models for cyberdeck builds, from Zero 2 W to Compute Module 4.
data.author: $ref:byline-neonpunk

children.text: The Raspberry Pi family offers excellent options for cyberdeck builds. Here's what you need to know about each model.
Pi Zero 2 W
The budget champion. 1GHz quad-core,512MB RAM, tiny form factor. Perfect for minimalist builds. Cons: No gigabit ethernet, limited USB ports.
Pi 4 Model B
The powerhouse. Up to 8GB RAM, gigabit ethernet, dual HDMI. Best for desktop replacement builds. Cons: Higher power draw, larger board.
Compute Module 4
The DIYer's choice. All the Pi 4 specs in a compact module designed for custom boards. Ideal for permanent, polished builds.

data.title: Display Options for Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: LCD, OLED, e-ink: comparing display technologies for cyberdeck projects.
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: Your display defines how you'll interact with your cyberdeck. Let's explore the options.
LCD Panels
Most common choice. 3.5" SPI screens are affordable ($15-25) and easy to interface. IPS panels offer better viewing angles.
OLED Displays
Vibrant colors, true blacks, thin profile. 1.3" and 2.42" OLED modules are popular. More expensive than LCD but the visual quality is unmatched.
E-Ink / E-Paper
The minimalist's choice. Extremely low power, sunlight readable, but slow refresh rates. Great for secondary displays or notification screens.

data.title: Mechanical Keyboards for Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: How to integrate mechanical keyboard switches into your cyberdeck build.
data.author: $ref:byline-soldermancer

children.text: The keyboard is arguably the most tactile element of your cyberdeck. Getting it right matters.
Switch Types
Cherry MX Red: Linear, quiet, good for gaming. Cherry MX Blue: Clicky, tactile, loud. Gateron Yellow: Budget-friendly linear. Kalih Brown: Tactile, middle ground.

data.title: Battery Power Systems for Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: Understanding LiPo batteries, 18650 cells, and power management for portable builds.
data.author: $ref:byline-soldermancer

children.text: Power is the lifeblood of your portable build. Let's talk batteries.
18650 Cells
The reliable choice. 3.7V nominal, 2000-3500mAh capacity. Protected cells recommended for DIY builds. Samsung, Sony, and LG are reputable brands.
LiPo Batteries
Higher capacity in smaller packages. Requires a Battery Management System (BMS) for safe charging. 3S or 4S configurations common for 12V+ systems.
Power Management
Always use a proper BMS. Set low-voltage cutoff to protect your cells. A USB-C PD sink controller like the FTDI PD sink can negotiate power delivery from modern chargers.

data.title: Mesh Networking with Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: Building resilient mesh networks using LoRa and other wireless technologies.
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: Mesh networking turns your cyberdeck into a node in a resilient communication network.
LoRa Radio
Long range, low power radio. RFM95W modules are popular for DIY projects. Can achieve 10km+ range in open areas with line of sight.
Software Options
Meshtastic: Open source, GPS-enabled, encrypted. Easy to get started. LibreMesh: More flexible, slightly steeper learning curve.

data.title: Operating Systems for Cyberdecks
data.excerpt: From Raspberry Pi OS to custom Linux distros, choosing the right OS for your build.
data.author: $ref:byline-neonpunk

children.text: Your choice of operating system affects everything from performance to battery life.
Raspberry Pi OS
The official OS. Debian-based, excellent hardware support, huge community. Good default choice for beginners.
Armbian
For Orange Pi and similar boards. Lightweight, performant, multiple desktop environment options. Ubuntu or Debian base.
Custom Minimal Distros
For power users: Alpine Linux, Arch Linux ARM, or building from scratch with Buildroot. Maximum control, steepest learning curve.

data.title: 3D Printing for Cyberdeck Builds
data.excerpt: An introduction to 3D printing your own cyberdeck enclosures and components.
data.author: $ref:byline-cabinetx

children.text: 3D printing opens up endless possibilities for custom cyberdeck enclosures. Here's how to get started.
Filament Types
PLA: Easy to print, biodegradable, not heat resistant. PETG: Stronger, more flexible, moderate difficulty. ASA: UV resistant, great for outdoor builds.

data.title: Soldering Fundamentals for Cyberdeck Builders
data.excerpt: Essential soldering techniques every cyberdeck builder needs to know.
data.author: $ref:byline-soldermancer

children.text: Good soldering makes the difference between a reliable build and a flaky one. Let's build your skills.
Essential Tools
Temperature-controlled iron (60W recommended), brass wool tip cleaner, leaded solder (for learning), lead-free solder (for final builds), flux pen, multimeter.
Through-Hole vs SMD
Through-hole components are easier to solder by hand and easier to debug. SMD (surface mount) components allow for smaller, more refined builds but require practice.
Common Mistakes
Too much heat: Destroying components or lifting pads. Insufficient heat: Cold joints that crack. Moving parts before solder sets: Creates brittle connections.

data.title: Hello from NeonPunk! First cyberdeck complete
data.author: $ref:byline-neonpunk

children.text: Just finished my first cyberdeck build after 6 months of on-and-off work. It's based on a Raspberry Pi 4 with a 7" touchscreen, Cherry MX Blue switches, and housed in a modified Pelican 1080 case. Runs Raspberry Pi OS with a custom DE that mimics a retro terminal aesthetic.
Total cost was around $280 including the power supply. Happy to answer any questions about the build process!

data.title: Help: Battery life only 2 hours on my Pi 4 build
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: My cyberdeck with a Pi 4 and 7" LCD is only getting about 2 hours of battery life from a 10000mAh LiPo pack. I have a 5V/3A buck converter feeding the Pi. Is this normal? Am I missing something?

data.title: Build Log: Mesh Networking Cyberdeck with LoRa
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: Starting my next build: a cyberdeck focused on mesh networking capabilities. The goal is to create a portable node that can operate independently of traditional infrastructure.
Components
Orange Pi 5 (RK3588), RFM95W LoRa module, 3.5" LCD, 18650 x4 pack, E-ink secondary display for status

data.title: Showcase: Workshop Terminal - Ruggedized Pi Compute Module 4 Build
data.author: $ref:byline-soldermancer

children.text: After months of iterative design, I'm proud to share my Workshop Terminal build. This is a ruggedized cyberdeck designed for actual work in my electronics repair shop.
Key features: IP67防水外壳 (waterproof case), integrated oscilloscope probe leads, serial console on multiple ports, 8 hour battery life, integrated multimeter inputs.

data.title: Reliable Parts Sources in 2025 - Vendor Roundup
data.author: $ref:byline-cabinetx

children.text: Let's compile a list of reliable vendors for cyberdeck components. I'll start:
Raspberry Pi: official Raspberry Pi Store or authorized resellers. Avoid third-party sellers with huge markups.
Orange Pi: OrangePi.org directly or AliExpress (choose reputable stores with reviews).

data.title: Virtual Meetup April 2025 - Share Your Workspace
data.author: $ref:byline-neonpunk

children.text: Join us for the April virtual meetup! This month's theme: Show us your workspace. Share photos of where you build, repair, and create.
April 20th, 2025 at 3PM UTC. Video call link will be posted here the day before.

data.title: Off Topic: Favorite Sci-Fi Cyberdeck Inspiration?
data.author: $ref:byline-glitchwitch

children.text: What fictional cyberdecks inspire your builds? For me, it's the portable terminals from Alien: Isolation and the data pads from Star Wars. Something about chunky, utilitarian design.
Welcome! That Pelican case mod looks clean. Did you use any heatset inserts for the ports, or just silicone seal around them?
Cherry MX Blues in a portable build - you're braver than me! Those things are loud. Looks great though, the custom DE really sells the aesthetic.
@GlitchWitch - I used gland seals for the USB and HDMI ports, same as you'd find on marine electrical boxes. Silicone on the battery compartment.
@SolderMancer - Ha! Yeah, I have a detachable keyboard option for when I'm in public. The sound profile is... not subtle.
2 hours from 10000mAh is actually pretty reasonable for a Pi 4 + LCD. The Pi 4 draws significant power under load. What's your converter efficiency? Cheap buck converters can lose 20-30%.
Have you considered underclocking? I run my Pi 4 at 1.5GHz instead of 1.8GHz and it makes a noticeable difference. Also, a more efficient converter like a TI LM2596 module rated for 3A would help.
Thanks for the suggestions! I'm going to try underclocking first. The converter I have is a generic Amazon one - I'll upgrade to a name-brand switching regulator.
Following this build log! The RK3588 is a beast - how are you handling thermals? That chip can get toasty.
For LoRa meshing, have you looked at Meshtastic? It's built on LoRa and has some excellent firmware that's well documented. Might save you some development time.
That is gorgeous work. Integrated multimeter inputs? That's next level. Are you using a specialized ADC or something off the shelf?
The aesthetic is perfect. Industrial but not sterile. What's the display - looks like a阳光直射 readable panel?
Great thread idea! I'd add: LCSC Electronics for components and small parts. Their prices are unbeatable and quality is consistent.
For 3D printer filament, eSUN has good quality at reasonable prices. Polymaker is a step up if you need better layer adhesion.
Count me in! My workspace is... let's say "enthusiastically organized chaos" right now. Time to clean up for the cameras.
I'll bring my Workshop Terminal for the meetup. Fair warning: it's covered in flux residue and solder splatter. Very authentic.
Blade Runner for me. That briefcase computer in the original film is the original cyberdeck aesthetic. Everything since has been iteration on that design.
The pip-boy from Fallout is a huge influence on my design philosophy. Form factor priorities: wearability > looks > specs. Even if it's historically inaccurate.
Join us for our monthly virtual meetup! This month's theme: Share Your Workspace. Show off your bench, your cyberdeck lair, or wherever you build. We'll have show-and-tell, Q&A, and a casual hangout.
In-person meetup in San Francisco! We'll be meeting at a maker space for an afternoon of show-and-tell, troubleshooting help, and community building.
March's theme was e-ink displays and low-power design. Thanks to everyone who presented their builds!
Our first European in-person meetup! A small group gathered in London for an afternoon of cyberdeck talk.

data.title: NeonPunk's Pelican Terminal
data.description: My first cyberdeck build. A Raspberry Pi 4 in a Pelican 1080 case with 7" touchscreen and Cherry MX Blue keyboard. Features custom retro terminal aesthetic.
data.parts_list: Raspberry Pi 4 (4GB), 7" LCD (1024x600), Cherry MX Blue x48, Pelican 1080 case, 10000mAh LiPo, PowerBoost 1000C, custom 3D printed inserts

data.title: GlitchWitch's Mesh Node
data.description: Work-in-progress build focused on mesh networking capabilities. Features LoRa radio, e-ink status display, and Orange Pi 5 compute module.
data.parts_list: Orange Pi 5 (RK3588), RFM95W LoRa module, 3.5" IPS LCD, 2.9" e-ink display, 18650 x4 battery pack, custom PCB

data.title: Workshop Terminal
data.description: A ruggedized, waterproof cyberdeck designed for use in an electronics repair workshop. Features integrated oscilloscope leads, serial console ports, and multimeter inputs.
data.parts_list: Compute Module 4 (8GB), 5" industrial LCD (1000 nit), IP67 case, 18650 x8 pack, precision instrumentation amps, Keithley-style probe connectors

data.title: The Traveller - Ultra Portable Build
data.description: A minimalist, ultra-portable cyberdeck designed for travel. Fits in a jacket pocket, runs for 12 hours on a single charge.
data.parts_list: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W, 2.8" LCD (SPI), custom mechanical keyboard (12 keys), 1000mAh LiPo, 3D printed PLA case

data.title: NUC Mobile Workstation
data.description: A desktop-class cyberdeck using an Intel NUC. For when you need actual horsepower on the go.
data.parts_list: Intel NUC 12 (i7), 10.1" portable HDMI monitor, KVM switch, 20000mAh power bank, briefcase enclosure

data.title: Leanto - Minimal Pi Zero Build
data.description: A minimal cyberdeck focused on the essentials. Pi Zero 2 W, small LCD, and a hand-wired keyboard matrix.
data.parts_list: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W, 3.5" SPI LCD, hand-wired keyboard (48 keys), 5000mAh power bank, cardboard case

data.hero_title:
Build the future.
For you.
By you.
data.hero_subtitle: 💅 the girls, gays, and theys are coming together: designing and creating cyberdecks that represent who they are, regardless of skill level — from first-timers to seasoned modders.
data.what_is_cyberdeck_text: A cyberdeck is a portable, custom-built computing rig that combines vintage aesthetics with modern technology. Think of it as a laptop you designed yourself — inside and out.
data.cta_text: Join our community of makers, hackers, and cyberdeck enthusiasts.
